---
title: "Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision"
chapter_id: "claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "spinoza"
  - "viea"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "aletheia"
  - "uat"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `spinoza`, `viea`, `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `spinoza`, `viea`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `spinoza`, `viea`, `coherence_exchange`; raw cache: `spinoza`, `viea`, `aletheia`, `uat`; pending source notes: `aletheia`, `uat`; connector/recovery: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

The architecture needs a durable epistemic layer for claims, evidence, contradictions, uncertainty, and revision.

Within the ASI Stack, this is an operational cognition layer problem. The layer receives governed goals, command contracts, memory packets, beliefs, work orders, and tool requests and must turn them into typed plans, context selections, claims, jobs, artifacts, approvals, and replayable execution records. The hard part is not naming the capability; it is making the boundary explicit enough that a later layer can test, replay, reject, or replace it without guessing what authority was assumed.

It inherits pressure from the previous chapter, Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy, and makes that pressure operational in a narrower boundary. It hands its output to Spinoza Verification and Proof-Carrying Claims, where the next layer tightens or implements the same constraint. That is why the chapter is written as part of a single stack rather than as a standalone paper summary.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A model can generate plausible prose without maintaining why it believes a claim or how new evidence changes prior beliefs.

In this chapter, the failure is more specific than bad prompting or weak model choice: a model can generate plausible prose without maintaining why it believes a claim or how new evidence changes prior beliefs. If `claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision` is left implicit, downstream layers inherit a capability whose authority, evidence burden, and rollback conditions are unclear. The stack therefore treats the missing contract as the defect, not merely the local model behavior.

This does not mean that every layer must be heavyweight. The stack is efficient precisely because small, typed artifacts can carry constraints forward while large models, tools, humans, or specialist modules are invoked only where the current boundary demands them.

## Core Claim

[claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Reasoning should maintain claim ledgers with support states, provenance, contradiction links, uncertainty, and revision history.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture, Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture, The Coherence Exchange, Aletheia Foundry, and Unified Adaptive Tribunal - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism turns the core claim into a working contract. First, extract claims into structured records. Second, attach evidence tier, provenance, and uncertainty. Third, revise beliefs when contradictions or stronger evidence appear.

- Extract claims into structured records.
- Attach evidence tier, provenance, and uncertainty.
- Revise beliefs when contradictions or stronger evidence appear.

Operationally, the layer should produce artifacts that can be inspected without rerunning the whole cognition path. A downstream reader or executor should be able to locate the relevant inputs, constraints, residuals, authority grants, and failure conditions. That artifact discipline is what lets the stack improve without making the improvement path opaque.

For implementation, the mechanism should be kept small at first: a schema, a ledger entry, a route decision, a context packet, a proof obligation, or a replayable job record. Only after that minimal form is testable should the layer absorb richer heuristics, learned routing, larger memory, or autonomous repair.

## Interfaces

The interface for Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision is the contract that lets adjacent layers compose without absorbing each other's authority. It should make the chapter's output legible to planning, memory, reasoning, execution, evidence, and improvement layers while preserving the boundary described by the core claim.

- VCM supplies source context.
- Spinoza verifies claims.
- Evidence matrix records book-level claims.

The upstream interface accepts the part-level inputs: governed goals, command contracts, memory packets, beliefs, work orders, and tool requests. The downstream interface emits typed plans, context selections, claims, jobs, artifacts, approvals, and replayable execution records. When either side is ambiguous, the layer should fail closed by producing a missing-contract, missing-evidence, or missing-authority record instead of silently continuing.

This interface also makes chapter insertion and reordering safer. A new chapter can be added when it introduces a genuinely new contract, invariant, or artifact type; otherwise it should merge into the layer that already owns the boundary.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Claims are separable from prose.
- Contradictions remain linked.
- Revision history is not overwritten.

If an invariant cannot be checked directly, the living-book version of the stack should at least record a proxy check and a residual. A weak proxy is acceptable in v0.2 when it is visible; it becomes dangerous only when the manuscript treats it as a completed proof.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Untracked assumptions.
- Belief inertia.
- Contradiction deletion.

For Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision, mitigation starts by turning each listed failure into a named reviewable artifact. When `Untracked assumptions` appears, the system should emit a residual, rejection, quarantine, regression, or human-review record rather than allowing the failure to disappear into later fluent output.

## Minimal implementation

A claim-record schema and belief-revision transition example.

The smallest useful implementation is deliberately narrow: A claim-record schema and belief-revision transition example. It should be easy to validate, easy to render in the book, and easy for a later coding run to convert into a schema, Lean predicate, deterministic fixture, trace table, or replayable command.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Claim extraction test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision boundary. | planned; not run |
| Contradiction detection test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision boundary. | planned; not run |
| Belief revision test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision boundary. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:claims.ledger.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A claim update preserves prior evidence and revision history. | planned |
| `lean:claims.ledger.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A contradiction prevents promotion until resolved, bounded, or recorded as residual. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `aletheia` | Aletheia Foundry | safe_general_intelligence_lineage | Safe general intelligence predecessor. Use ideas; avoid final branding if collision remains. | local raw cache available |
| `uat` | Unified Adaptive Tribunal | evaluation_refinement | Multi-AI collaborative refinement/evaluation protocol. | local raw cache available |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision defines one boundary in the ASI Stack: Reasoning should maintain claim ledgers with support states, provenance, contradiction links, uncertainty, and revision history. Its job is to make a capability more governable, not merely more impressive.

In v0.2, this chapter gives Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision a complete architectural boundary: the architecture needs a durable epistemic layer for claims, evidence, contradictions, uncertainty, and revision. Its next maturity step is to substantiate the boundary through `VCM supplies source context`, preserve `Claims are separable from prose`, and replace planned proof/test hooks with validated artifacts. Claims should move only when source notes, code, proofs, or run records justify the movement.
